text.title: VUI Design Process
text.summary: Six-step process for designing voice user interfaces, from use-case selection through Wizard of Oz validation.

rule.statement: Select voice for hands-free tasks, quick commands, simple queries, accessibility needs, and multitasking contexts only — not for complex data entry, private information, or browsing.
rule.rationale: Voice adds a second interaction modality with significant design cost; applying it where it has no advantage over touch or keyboard wastes that investment.

section.title: Step 2: Write Sample Dialogues

rule.statement: Write natural conversation scripts for happy path and top 3 error paths before designing any intent schema or building any NLU model.
rule.rationale: Dialogue-first design grounds the schema in real language. Schema-first design produces idealized utterances that do not match natural speech, leading to poor NLU coverage in production.

section.p: Example happy path: User: "Set a timer for 15 minutes" / System: "Timer set for 15 minutes, starting now."
Example error path: User: "Order pizza" / System: "Sure! Which pizza place? You've ordered from Papa's Pizza and Domino's before."

section.title: Step 3: Define Intents and Slots

rule.statement: Define each intent with required and optional slots, then generate at least 10 utterance variations per intent before NLU training; 20+ is the practical minimum for production.
rule.rationale: Underrepresented utterance variation is the primary cause of NLU production failures. Agents generating training data produce syntactically varied but semantically homogeneous utterances — add real user quotes from research.

section.p: Intent structure: name, description, required slots (with reprompt if missing), optional slots, sample utterances, response template.

rule.statement: Keep system prompts under 20 words for smart speakers, under 15 for IVR, under 30 for screen+voice hybrids; read every prompt aloud before approving.
rule.rationale: Text that reads naturally in a document is often awkward when spoken; prompt length limits enforce spoken-word conventions.

rule.statement: Use implicit confirmation for low-risk actions and explicit confirmation for irreversible ones — never confirm every action (users abandon verbose confirmation flows).
rule.rationale: Confirmation overhead is the leading cause of voice feature abandonment; calibrate confirmation strategy to action risk, not to developer anxiety.

rule.statement: Design a 3-attempt error recovery chain: 1st attempt rephrase the prompt; 2nd attempt offer examples; 3rd attempt offer a fallback path (agent transfer, visual alternative, or different action).
rule.rationale: A missing third-attempt fallback leaves users stranded with no exit; they abandon the feature and do not return.

list.item: Error types to handle: no input, speech not recognized, intent not understood, slot value invalid, confirmation denied
Never use "I'm sorry" as a standalone response — it acknowledges the error without offering resolution
Never use URLs in voice prompts — they are unusable in audio context

rule.statement: Run Wizard of Oz testing (human playing system role) before any NLU training — intent schemas generated without real utterance data have poor production coverage.
rule.rationale: WoZ testing captures natural speech variation, accent patterns, and implicit slot omissions that agent-generated utterance sets miss entirely.

rule.statement: Test with at least 3 different accents and 2 ambient noise levels before shipping to production.
rule.rationale: STT recognition accuracy varies significantly across accents and noise levels; lab testing in a quiet studio is not representative of real-world usage.

text.reference: Prompt design template with reprompt levels
Linter for dialogue scripts: prompt length, anti-patterns
